Name: jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping: Training-Free Inference Acceleration via Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: Layer skipping in neural networks can be dynamically optimized during inference by leveraging the spectral stability of Jacobian matrices, allowing for significant latency reduction without retraining or compromising accuracy.
Related Work: Existing methods for layer skipping often rely on static heuristics or costly calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) dynamically allocates numerical precision based on activation geometry, but does not address layer skipping. Our proposal distinguishes itself by directly targeting the spectral stability of Jacobian matrices to dynamically skip layers during inference, ensuring minimal perturbation and strict latency budgets.
Abstract: We propose Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping (JBLS), a training-free framework that dynamically optimizes layer skipping during inference by leveraging the spectral stability of Jacobian matrices. Our hypothesis is that the spectral stability of the Jacobian matrix at each layer serves as a direct proxy for the layer's contribution to the final output, allowing for dynamic layer skipping without retraining or compromising accuracy. JBLS computes the spectral stability of the Jacobian matrix on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping it to a layer-skipping policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the framework with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral stability to generalization stability under layer skipping. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, JBLS achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, JBLS provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate JBLS on WILDS benchmarks to measure throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines.
Assess the impact of JBLS on latency and memory usage across different neural network architectures.
Validate the spectral stability hypothesis by measuring the correlation between Jacobian spectral stability and layer contribution to final output.
Test the robustness of JBLS under structured perturbations and domain shifts, quantifying stability radii.
Compare the computational overhead of JBLS with existing training-free inference acceleration methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of on-the-fly Jacobian spectral stability computation may offset latency gains.
JBLS may not be effective for architectures with highly interdependent layers.
The framework relies on the assumption that spectral stability is a reliable proxy for layer contribution, which may not hold for all tasks.
Dynamic layer skipping could introduce instability in the inference process, particularly for tasks requiring high precision.
